HEAX: An Architecture for Computing on Encrypted Data

PDF (text-layer), arXiv:1909.09731 · full view

Motivation and Contributions

Cloud computing introduces serious data-privacy and confidentiality risks, and regulations such as GDPR, HIPAA, and CCPA make it impossible for some data owners to outsource computation in the clear. Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE), introduced by Gentry in 2009, lets a cloud server evaluate arbitrary functions directly on ciphertexts without ever decrypting them, giving end-to-end encryption where only the client (holding the secret key) can recover results. The central obstacle to deploying FHE at scale is its enormous computation overhead compared to plaintext computation.

HEAX (Homomorphic Encryption Acceleration) is a hardware architecture targeting this overhead. The paper focuses on the CKKS scheme, which performs approximate computation on real/fixed-point numbers and supports efficient truncation, making it the prime candidate for machine learning on encrypted data (for example oblivious neural-network inference in MLaaS). The authors are from UC San Diego and Microsoft Research; the work appeared at ASPLOS 2020.

Three main contributions:

  • A novel, highly parallelizable architecture for the Number-Theoretic Transform (NTT) that processes arbitrary-sized polynomials with adjustable throughput. NTT is the usual bottleneck across lattice-based cryptosystems, so the design is of independent interest.
  • The first hardware architecture for the CKKS homomorphic encryption scheme, leveraging multi-layer parallelism from the ciphertext level down to fine-grained modular-arithmetic engines, and scalable across different FPGA chips because it is not custom-built for one fixed parameter set.
  • A proof-of-concept on two Intel FPGAs (Arria 10 and Stratix 10), implementing all high-level CKKS operations and evaluated across three FHE parameter sets, demonstrating more than two orders of magnitude speedup over the heavily optimized Microsoft SEAL library on CPU.

Encoding/decoding and encryption/decryption are cheap and stay on the client side; HEAX accelerates the computationally intensive server-side operations: addition, multiplication, key switching, relinearization, rescaling, and rotation.

Design Challenges and Prior Art

A ciphertext in FHE is a set (usually a pair) of polynomials of degree n-1 (vectors of n integers) modulo a large integer. Homomorphic operations involve computationally intensive modular arithmetic on big integers (hundreds of bits) with convoluted data dependencies, and the polynomial degree n is large (thousands), so storing entire intermediate results on the FPGA chip is prohibitive.

Prior FPGA work for non-CKKS schemes took one of three approaches, each with drawbacks: (i) co-processors that accelerate only low-level ring operations while high-level operations stay on the CPU, limiting practical use; (ii) storing intermediate results in off-chip memory, which degrades performance so severely it can be worse than naive software execution; or (iii) hardware fixed to a single modest parameter (e.g. n = 2^12), even though encryption parameters determine both security level and multiplication depth and are application dependent. A primary HEAX design goal is therefore an architecture usable across a wide range of parameters, together with techniques to efficiently store/access data on-chip and minimize (or for some parameter sets eliminate) off-chip memory accesses.

CKKS Preliminaries: RNS, Gadget Decomposition, Subroutines

CKKS supports homomorphic addition (cheap: add corresponding polynomials) and multiplication (expensive: it increases the number of polynomial components, requiring relinearization to bring the ciphertext back to a pair). To keep underlying plaintext magnitudes from blowing up, a rescaling operation divides the plaintext value by a constant. SIMD-style packing embeds many numbers in one ciphertext, and rotation permutes those numbers. Relinearization, rescaling, and rotation can all be expressed as a unified operation called Key Switching plus pre/post-processing.

Notation: integers/reals in normal case, polynomials/vectors in bold (a), vectors of polynomials/matrices in upper-case bold (A). n is a power of two; the polynomial ring is R = Z[X]/(X^n + 1), and R_q = R/qR is the residue ring modulo integer q. The product u . v is reduced mod X^n+1 and mod q; the symbol for coefficient-wise (dyadic) multiplication is the Hadamard-style product.

Residue Number System (RNS). Choosing q as a product of pairwise-coprime integers p_i lets arithmetic over R_q be carried out independently and in parallel on each RNS basis R_{p_i} via a ring isomorphism. HEAX exploits this RNS-level parallelism extensively. The full-RNS variant of CKKS underlies the design.

Gadget decomposition. An RNS-friendly gadget decomposition g^{-1} maps an element of R_q into a vector of small polynomials in R^d such that a = inner-product(g, A) mod q.

CKKS subroutines used: Setup, SymEnc, KeyGen, KskGen (key-switching-key generation), and Add. Two frequently used low-level primitives are modular reduction Mod(x, p) (Barrett-style, using a precomputed u = floor(2^{2w}/p)) and the optimized modular multiplication MulRed below.

Optimized Modular Multiplication (Algorithm 1)

The core fine-grained arithmetic primitive. For w-bit words and a modulus p < 2^{w-2}, given x, y in Z_p and a precomputed y' = floor(y . 2^w / p), it computes x . y mod p:

Algorithm 1  Optimized Modular Mult. | MulRed(x, y, y', p)
Input:  x, y in Z_p,  p < 2^(w-2),  and  y' = floor(y . 2^w / p)
Output: z <- x . y  (mod p)
1: z   <- x . y      (mod 2^w)      > the lower word of the product
2: t   <- floor(x . y' / 2^w)       > the upper word of the product
3: z_e <- t . p      (mod 2^w)      > the lower word of the product
4: z   <- z - z_e                   > single-word subtraction
5: if z >= p then
6:    z <- z - p
7: end if

MULT Module and Word Size

Homomorphic multiplication is performed in RNS and NTT form. Although ciphertexts can in general have more than two components, in practice they are relinearized and multiplied on two components; the HEAX MULT architecture is nonetheless generic for any number of components. The homomorphic multiplication is given in Algorithm 2, where the inputs are two ciphertexts and the output is a three-component ciphertext, computed independently on each RNS basis.

Algorithm 2  Homomorphic Mult. | CKKS.Mul(ct0, ct1)
Input:  ct0 = (A0~, A1~),  ct1 = (B0~, B1~)  in (prod_{i=0..l} R_{p_i})^2
Output: ct  = (C0~, C1~, C2~)               in (prod_{i=0..l} R_{p_i})^3
1: for (i = 0; i <= l; i = i + 1) do
2:    c0,i~ <- Mod( a0,i~ (*) b0,i~ , p_i )              > Dyadic Core
3:    c1,i~ <- Mod( a0,i~ (*) b1,i~ + a1,i~ (*) b0,i~ , p_i )
4:    c2,i~ <- Mod( a1,i~ (*) b1,i~ , p_i )
5: end for

Here (*) denotes coefficient-wise (dyadic) product. Since ciphertexts are in NTT form by default, homomorphic multiplication reduces to a series of dyadic products over components.

Word size. SEAL uses 64-bit native operations for x86, but on FPGAs the DSP bit-width varies (the two boards here have 27-bit DSPs). A naive 64-bit multiplier needs nine 27-bit DSPs, whereas a 54-bit multiplier needs only four. Reducing RNS-base bit-width may increase the number of bases by roughly 64/54 ~ 1.2x, but in practice small ciphertext moduli are under 54 bits so no extra moduli are needed. Switching from 64-bit to 54-bit native operations yields a 1.4x to 2.25x reduction in DSP count depending on HE parameters. To support 54-bit words, all moduli p_i must be (i) less than 52 bits to keep Algorithm 1 correct and (ii) congruent to 1 mod 2n to support NTT; SEAL was modified and such moduli precomputed for every parameter set.

MULT architecture. The MULT module handles both ciphertext-ciphertext (C-C) and ciphertext-plaintext (C-P, a special case) multiplication. It contains nc_DYD Dyadic Cores, computing nc_DYD dyadic multiplications per clock cycle. Each Dyadic core takes two coefficients (Op1, Op2), two precomputed constants (R1, R2), and the one-word prime p. For ciphertexts with alpha and beta components, the output has alpha + beta - 1 components. To bound BRAM use while keeping data transfer low, HEAX allocates alpha memories of size n for ct0 and beta for ct1, achieving O((alpha+beta).n) data transfer and BRAM consumption. Because each BRAM supports only one read and one write per cycle, each polynomial is stored across nc_DYD parallel memory blocks sharing common read/write address signals; one row across these BRAMs is a memory element (ME). Each cycle one ME is read from ct0 and ct1 banks (ME1/ME2) and the result (ME3) is written to a separate output memory.

NTT and Flooring Algorithms (Algorithms 3 and 4)

NTT and its inverse (INTT) are the most computationally intensive low-level operations. Polynomial multiplication is done via the convolution theorem: c = a . b in R_p corresponds to the negacyclic convolution of coefficient vectors. For a ring degree n, a prime p = 1 mod 2n is chosen so a 2n-th primitive root of unity psi exists. Polynomials are kept in NTT form to reduce the number of conversions. HEAX adapts the fast negacyclic-convolution NTT of Longa and Naehrig.

Algorithm 3  Number-Theoretic Transform (NTT) | NTT_p(a)
Input:  a in Z_p^n,  p == 1 (mod 2n),  Y in Z_p^n storing powers of psi
        in bit-reverse order, and Y' = floor(Y . 2^w / p).
Output: a~ <- NTT_p(a)  in bit-reverse ordering.
1: for (m = 1; m < n; m = 2m) do
2:    for (i = 0; i < m; i++) do
3:       for (j = i.n/m; j < (2i+1).n/(2m); j++) do
4:          v        = MulRed( a_{j + n/m}, y_{m+i}, y'_{m+i}, p )
5:          a_{j+n/m} = a_j - v   (mod p)
6:          a_j       = a_j + v   (mod p)        > NTT Core
7:       end for
8:    end for
9: end for
10: a~ <- a

A related operation used during key switching and rescaling is flooring, which divides by a prime in the RNS/NTT domain:

Algorithm 4  RNS Flooring | Floor(C~, p)
Input:  C~ = (c0~, ..., c_{l+1}~) in Z_{p0}^n x ... x Z_{pl}^n x Z_p^n.
Output: C'~ = (c'0~, ..., c'l~)  in Z_{p0}^n x ... x Z_{pl}^n.
1: a <- INTT_p(c_{l+1}~)                          > INTT Module
2: for (i = 0; i <= l; i = i + 1) do
3:    r  <- Mod(a, p_i)
4:    r~ <- NTT_{p_i}(r)                            > NTT Module
5:    c'i~ <- c_i~ - r~        (mod p_i)
6:    c'i~ <- Mod( [p^{-1}]_{p_i} . c'i~ , p_i )    > MS Module
7: end for

NTT Architecture and Multi-Level Parallelism

HEAX uses NTT to mean both NTT and INTT modules. In KeySwitch (Algorithm 5) NTT is used in several places with differing throughput needs, so HEAX allocates one NTT module per NTT operation and makes each module's throughput adjustable via its number of cores nc_NTT. The module computes an n-point NTT in log n stages, each stage taking n/(2 nc_NTT) steps; the full NTT takes (n log n)/(2 nc_NTT) cycles.

NTT Core. Each core accepts two coefficients (c_in.a, c_in.b), one twiddle factor w, one precomputed value wp, and a prime p, and outputs two transformed coefficients (c_out.a, c_out.b). All modular operations inside the core are pipelined. At full utilization, 2 nc_NTT coefficients are transformed per clock cycle. To read/write that many coefficients per cycle, each polynomial is stored across many parallel BRAMs sharing read/write addresses, exploiting the fact that NTT's access counts are always powers of two.

Access patterns (Type 1 vs Type 2). During the first (log n - log nc_NTT - 1) stages (Type 1), the paired coefficients for each NTT core lie in different MEs, so two MEs must be fetched before computation, naively introducing a 50% pipeline bubble. After n/2^i reaches 2 nc_NTT, the inter-ME dependency vanishes and pairs come from within one ME (Type 2), but coefficients must be reordered to reach the right core. Twiddle factors have their own pattern: at stage i only 2^i unique values are used; they are stored in batches of size nc_NTT in parallel.

Optimal multiplexers. Naive reordering would use 2 nc_NTT multiplexers each selecting from 2 nc_NTT inputs (MUX_{2nc_NTT}), which consumes huge register/logic resources and fails place-and-route beyond about 32 cores. Instead HEAX observes that each NTT-core input draws from at most log nc_NTT distinct indices, so it uses (4 . nc_NTT) multiplexers of size at most MUX_{log 2nc_NTT}. For an 8-core NTT module this reduces register usage from 224,000 to 97,000. Since register is the most limited resource for the NTT module, this optimization is essential to scale the design.

Full pipeline utilization. To remove the Type-1 bubble, HEAX doubles the ME size (storing 2 nc_NTT consecutive coefficients per ME) and halves memory depth, so two MEs can be transformed in the next two cycles after the two reads, yielding full NTT-core utilization. All reads/writes across stages are in-place, so no extra BRAM stores intermediates.

Memory utilization and word-packing. M20K units hold 512 words of 40 bits and serve one access per cycle. As long as n/beta >= 512, M20K is fully depth-utilized (only marginal at n = 2^12, nc_NTT = 16, which is not BRAM-constrained). Width-wise, storing one 54-bit coefficient per BRAM only reaches 54/(2.40) = 68% utilization; packing multiple coefficients reaches over 98% for beta = 8, which matters because at n = 2^14 the design becomes BRAM-constrained and must spill to DRAM. The INTT module is identical to NTT except the INTT core replaces the NTT core, the control unit runs stages in reverse, and twiddle factors are the inverse ones.

Key Switching Algorithm (Algorithm 5)

Key switching makes a ciphertext decryptable under a different secret key, homomorphically. It is the most computationally intensive high-level CKKS operation and underlies relinearization and rotation. HEAX uses a gadget decomposition (with the special modulus p and a rescaling at the end) that yields fast, almost noise-free key switching. The procedure, with its INTT, NTT, dyadic-product, and modulus-switching phases marked, is:

Algorithm 5  Key Switching | KeySwitch(ct, ksk)
Input:  ct  = (C0~, C1~) in (prod_{i=0..l} R_{p_i})^2,  and
        ksk = ( (Di,0~)_{0<=i<=L+1} | (Di,1~)_{0<=i<=L+1} )
              in (p . prod_{i=0..L} R_{p_i})^{(L+2) x 2}
Output: ct' = (C0'~, C1'~) in (prod_{i=0..l} R_{p_i})^2
 1: for (i = 0; i <= l; i = i + 1) do
 2:    a <- INTT_{p_i}(c1,i~)                              > INTT Module
 3:    for (j = 0; j <= l; j = j + 1) do
 4:       if i != j then
 5:          b  <- Mod(a, p_j)
 6:          b~ <- NTT_{p_j}(b)                              > NTT Module
 7:       else
 8:          b~ <- a~
 9:       end if
10:       c''0,j~ <- c''0,j~ + b~ (*) d_i,0,j~  (mod p_j)
11:       c''1,j~ <- c''1,j~ + b~ (*) d_i,1,j~  (mod p_j)   > Dyadic Mod.
12:    end for
13:    b  <- Mod(a, p)
14:    b~ <- NTT_p(b)                                       > NTT Module
15:    c''0,l+1~ <- c''0,l+1~ + b~ (*) d0,i,L+1~  (mod p_j)
16:    c''1,l+1~ <- c''1,l+1~ + b~ (*) d1,i,L+1~  (mod p_j) > Dyd. M.
17: end for
18: ct' <- ( Floor(C0''~, p), Floor(C1''~, p) )            > INTT/NTT/MS
19: ct' <- CKKS.Add(ct, ct')

Here (*) is the dyadic product and ksk = (D0 | D1) is the key-switching (relinearization / Galois) key.

KeySwitch Architecture and Throughput Balancing

KeySwitch is designed as a fully end-to-end pipelined module that processes many key-switching operations simultaneously without excessive FIFOs. To reduce on-chip memory, the design takes one polynomial (one RNS component) at a time and outputs two polynomials. Since all polynomials are in NTT form by default, the input is first converted back via the first INTT module (INTT0), then transformed to NTT form for all other primes including the special modulus. Because each INTT requires k NTTs (k = L + 1 = number of RNS components), the NTT throughput must be k times that of INTT0. This is realized either by one NTT module with k-times more cores, or by multiple NTT modules with fewer cores each (this NTT block is denoted NTT0). After NTT, the DyadMult module multiplies by the relinearization/Galois keys; a separate Dyadic module also handles the dyadic product on the original input polynomial.

The flow repeats k times (one per RNS component), accumulating results in two sets of BRAM banks (one per output polynomial). Then Modulus Switching (executing Floor) transforms the special-modulus polynomial back to the time domain (INTT1) and forward under every other prime (NTT1), multiplies by the inverse prime, and subtracts; the MS module embeds the multiply and subtract. Outputs are two sets of k polynomials (Output Poly 0/1).

Throughput balancing. With m0 NTT0 modules, nc_NTT0 = k . nc_INTT0 / m0. The Dyadic module throughput must meet or exceed NTT's, giving nc_DYD = 4 nc_NTT0 / log n. Similarly nc_INTT1 = ceil(nc_INTT0 / k), nc_NTT1 = nc_INTT0, and nc_MS = ceil((2 nc_NTT1)/log n). Optimal per-board parameters are summarized in the paper's Table 5 (see Results).

Pipeline and synchronization. All modules and their internal components are pipelined and throughput-balanced, so multiple key switches occupy different pipeline stages simultaneously. The fifth Dyadic module (on the input polynomial) is synchronized with the others to avoid memory replication of the large key banks, which creates Data Dependency 1: by the time the k-th Dyadic module starts, the input poly may be overwritten by the next operation, so HEAX allocates f1 = ceil(3 + nc_INTT0/nc_NTT0) polynomial buffers. Data Dependency 2 arises because MS consumes DyadMult outputs, requiring f2 = ceil(1 + m0 . nc_INTT1/nc_NTT1 + nc_INTT1 . log n / nc_MS) buffers.

System View, Memory, and Data Flow

The system comprises a host CPU and an FPGA board connected over PCIe; the board holds the FPGA chip and off-chip DRAM over a DDR interface. On the FPGA chip sit the MULT, KeySwitch, NTT, and INTT modules plus a control unit and shell. The host runs SEAL-based applications with a scheduler and a memory map.

On-chip vs off-chip. Off-chip DRAM has gigabytes of capacity but high latency; on-chip BRAM has only megabits but very high throughput. A primary goal is to avoid off-chip access. Thanks to minimal-BRAM techniques, BRAM reuse, and data compaction, no off-chip access is needed for the n = 2^12 set on either board. For n = 2^13 there is enough on-chip memory on Stratix 10. For n = 2^14 there is not enough BRAM, so some data must go to DRAM. HEAX places the key-switching keys (ksk) in DRAM because their size grows fastest (roughly O(n.k^2), and since k grows roughly linearly with n, almost O(n^3)) and each ksk element is read only once per KeySwitch, whereas each twiddle factor is read k times. The ksk is distributed across four DRAM banks and read in burst mode, fully pipelined; the authors verify DRAM bandwidth suffices (for n = 2^14, k = 8, 64-bit words, about 151 megabits must stream in 383 microseconds, requiring > 49.28 GBps, below the combined four-channel bandwidth). DRAM can also cache results destined for reuse, addressed via the host-side memory map without PCIe round trips.

PCIe transfer. Computation and transfer are interleaved. Transfers use DMA over pinned memory; to maximize PCIe throughput, at least a complete polynomial (2^15 to 2^17 bytes) is sent per request, and eight threads interleave eight polynomials at a time. MULT uses double buffering; KeySwitch needs quadruple buffering due to the input-polynomial dependency, and writing stalls if a buffer has not yet been read.

Implementation, FPGAs, and Parameters

HEAX was implemented on two Intel FPGAs representing different resource classes. Board-A is an Arria 10 GX 1150 (1518 DSPs, 1.71M REG, 427K ALM, 53 Mb / 2.7K M20K BRAM, 2 DRAM channels at 34 GBps). Board-B is a Stratix 10 GX 2800 (5760 DSPs, 3.73M REG, 933K ALM, 229 Mb / 11.7K M20K BRAM, 4 DRAM channels at 64 GBps). DSPs perform one 27-bit (or two 18-bit) multiplications; each M20K holds 512 words of 40 bits.

Security derives directly from CKKS (unchanged functionality) and uses the HE security standard parameters for 128-bit classical security; changing the word size and using RNS-level parallelism do not affect security. Three parameter sets are evaluated, with n the polynomial size, the total ciphertext-modulus bit-length floor(log qp)+1, and k the number of RNS components:

HE Param. Set     n        floor(log qp)+1     k
Set-A           2^12           109            2
Set-B           2^13           218            4
Set-C           2^14           438            8

Sets at n = 2^11 or below are almost never used (multiplication depth 0 or 1), and n = 2^15+ causes enormous blow-up, so these three span the practical range.

The KeySwitch architecture instantiated per board/parameter set (Table 5) shows the multi-level structure explicitly, where X(c) denotes X module(s) with c cores each:

Arria10,  n=2^12 (Set-A):
  1 x INTT(8)  -> 2 x NTT(8)  -> 3 x Dyad(4)
  -> 2 x INTT(4) -> 2 x NTT(8)  -> 2 x MS(2)
Stratix10, n=2^12 (Set-A):
  1 x INTT(16) -> 2 x NTT(16) -> 3 x Dyad(8)
  -> 2 x INTT(8) -> 2 x NTT(16) -> 2 x MS(4)
Stratix10, n=2^13 (Set-B):
  1 x INTT(16) -> 4 x NTT(16) -> 5 x Dyad(8)
  -> 2 x INTT(4) -> 2 x NTT(16) -> 2 x MS(4)
Stratix10, n=2^14 (Set-C):
  1 x INTT(8)  -> 4 x NTT(16) -> 5 x Dyad(8)
  -> 2 x INTT(1) -> 2 x NTT(8)  -> 2 x MS(4)

Resource Consumption

Per-core resources and pipeline depth (Table 3): the Dyadic core uses 22 DSP, 4526 REG, 1663 ALM, 23 stages; the NTT core 10 DSP, 6297 REG, 2066 ALM, 50 stages; the INTT core 10 DSP, 5449 REG, 2119 ALM, 49 stages.

Module resources scale primarily in DSP/REG/ALM and M20K count with the number of cores (BRAM bits are roughly fixed per module). Selected entries from Table 4 (BRAM reported for Set-B, n = 2^13):

Module  #Cores  DSP   REG     ALM     BRAM #M20K   Cycles
MULT      4      88   42817   15795       65        1024
MULT      8     176   61878   22160       65         512
MULT     16     352   93594   35257      164         128
MULT     32     704  181503   62157      293          64
NTT       4      40   61670   22316       86        6144
NTT       8      80   96919   36336      185        3072
NTT      16     160  196205   67865      380        1536
NTT      32     320  387357  142300      725         768
INTT      4      40   63917   22700       86        6144
INTT      8      80  104575   37331      185        3072
INTT     16     160  182478   68645      380        1536
INTT     32     320  384267  144957      724         768

Whole-design utilization (Table 6) reaches, for example on Stratix 10 Set-B: 2610 DSP (45%), 1.98M REG (53%), 698,884 ALM (75%), BRAM bits 84%, M20K 88%, at 300 MHz. Arria 10 Set-A uses 1185 DSP (78%) at 275 MHz. The maximum clock frequency after eliminating critical paths is 275 MHz (Arria 10) and 300 MHz (Stratix 10). HEAX can be auto-instantiated at different scales with no manual tuning: the Stratix 10 instantiation of Set-A consumes about twice the resources and delivers about twice the throughput of the Arria 10 instantiation.

Performance and Benchmarks

HEAX is compared against Microsoft SEAL V3.3 (single-threaded Intel Xeon Silver 4108 at 1.80 GHz; SEAL is thread-safe but not multithreaded, and CKKS evaluation is largely non-parallelizable, e.g. Modulus Switching is serial). Numbers below are operations per second.

Low-level operations (Table 7), CPU vs HEAX and speedup:

FPGA / Set     NTT                INTT               Dyadic MULT
               CPU    HEAX   x    CPU    HEAX   x     CPU     HEAX     x
Arria10  A    7222   89518 12.4  7568   89518 11.8  36931  1074219  29.1
Stratix  A    7222  195313 27.0  7568  195313 25.8  36931  1171875  31.7
Stratix  B    3437   90144 26.2  3539   90144 25.5  18362   585938  31.9
Stratix  C    1631   41853 25.7  1659   41853 25.2   9117   292969  32.1

High-level operations (Table 8) - the headline results:

FPGA / Set     KeySwitch                  MULT+ReLin
               CPU   HEAX    Speed-up      CPU   HEAX    Speed-up
Arria10  A     488   44759    91.7         420   44759   106.6
Stratix  A     488   97656   200.5         420   97656   232.5
Stratix  B      97   22536   232.3          84   22536   268.3
Stratix  C      16    2616   163.5          15    2616   174.4

On Arria 10 HEAX reaches close to two orders of magnitude speedup; on Stratix 10 it achieves 164x to 268x improvement across parameter sets for the complete high-level operations.

Versus GPUs (Table 9), NTT operations per second: HEAX on Stratix 10 (ten 16-core NTT modules) vs NVIDIA Tesla K80 (2496 cores) and Tesla P100 (3584 cores):

Poly Size   HEAX(10x16)   Tesla-K80   Tesla-P100   Comparison
  2^12        1953130       25641       27777        70-76x
  2^13         901440       20833       25000        36-43x
  2^14         418530        5181       11494        36-81x

HEAX is 36x to 81x faster than data-center GPUs for NTT while consuming significantly less power.

Related Work and Conclusion

No prior FPGA or GPU hardware existed for CKKS, so HEAX is the first; comparison to designs for other (non-CKKS) schemes is not strictly fair. Prior FPGA work for BFV (e.g. for n = 2^15) suffered from massive off-chip transfers and did not beat CPUs. The closest work, Roy et al., built a BFV architecture on Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ ZCU102, avoiding off-chip access by fixing n = 2^12 and reporting 13x over FV-NFLlib (which is itself 1.2x slower than SEAL); HEAX is far more modular and scalable, instantiated for three parameter sets with no manual tuning. Other lines include FPGA co-processors accelerating single operations (e.g. HEPU for LTV, 3.2x-4.4x), very large integer multipliers (768K to 1.18M-bit) for FHE-over-integers, GPU accelerations of various schemes, and YASHE/LTV accelerators, though YASHE and LTV rest on an overstretched NTRU assumption that is broken by a subfield-lattice attack and are no longer secure. Some prior YASHE designs assumed unlimited memory bandwidth (unrealistic) or relied on Cached-NTT; HEAX instead exploits the RNS ring-isomorphism to do independent per-component computation, avoiding off-chip access for small parameters and minimizing it for large ones.

Conclusion. HEAX is the first fully implemented hardware accelerator for the CKKS FHE scheme, the prime candidate for machine learning on encrypted data. Its components are reusable for other lattice-based cryptosystems and FHE/HE schemes, and the architecture is flexible across FPGA chips and not tied to a fixed parameter set, demonstrating more than two orders of magnitude speedup over optimized CPU software across a wide range of FHE parameters. The authors hope HEAX paves the way for large-scale deployment of privacy-preserving cloud computation.
